front: Reaction: McMurry reaction: titanium(III) chloride reductively couples carbonyls to form alkenes (variant of pinacol coupling)
Reaction: Wittig reaction: phosphonium ylides react with ketones/aldehydes; favors Z-stereoisomer
Reaction: Peterson olefination: alpha-silyl carbanion added to carbonyl, followed by elimination
Reaction: Julia reaction: phenyl sulfones react with carbonyl compounds, reduced with sodium amalgam
Reaction: Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons (HWE) and Still-Gennari modification: stabilized phosphonate anion + carbonyl; Still-Gennari favors Z-alkenes using KHMDS and 18-crown-6
Reaction: Zaitsev's rule: elimination reactions favor the more-substituted alkene (opposite of Hofmann's rule)
Reaction: Shapiro, Bamford-Stevens, and Chugaev/Grieco eliminations also produce alkenes
Reaction: Grubbs catalyst (ruthenium-based): preferred for metathesis due to air/water stability; second generation contains NHC ligand
Reaction: Schrock catalyst (molybdenum-based): more active than Grubbs but less stable
Reaction: Metathesis involves formation of a metallacyclobutane intermediate
Reaction: Carbenes (neutral carbon with 6 valence electrons) coordinated to Ru in Grubbs catalyst
Reaction: Markovnikov's rule: addition of HX/water gives the more-substituted product
Reaction: Anti-Markovnikov: hydroboration-oxidation (BH3 in THF) gives anti-Markovnikov alcohol
Reaction: Oxymercuration-demercuration: mercury acetate + water, then NaBH4; Markovnikov alcohol without rearrangements
Reaction: Bromine test: alkenes decolorize a solution of liquid bromine (canonical test for unsaturation)
Reaction: Epoxidation: peroxyacids convert alkenes to epoxides; Shi epoxidation uses ketone catalyst + Oxone
Reaction: Sharpless asymmetric dihydroxylation: OsO4 + chiral quinine ligand → diols
Reaction: Prevost and Woodward reactions: iodine + silver benzoate/acetate for diols
Reaction: Carbenes add to alkenes to make cyclopropanes
Reaction: Radical initiators (peroxides) reverse regioselectivity: anti-Markovnikov HBr addition
Reaction: Alkene acts as the dienophile reacting with a conjugated diene to form cyclohexene
Reaction: Woodward-Hoffmann rules explain stereochemistry of pericyclic reactions including Diels-Alder
Reaction: Bredt's rule: double bond cannot be placed at the bridgehead of bridged rings (angle/ring strain)
Concept: Dewar-Chatt-Duncanson model: describes how alkenes donate sigma electrons to metals and accept pi electrons (backbonding)
Concept: Zeise's salt: platinum bonded to three chlorines + one ethylene; first organometallic compound
Concept: Pi backbonding increases bond length of coordinated alkenes compared to free alkenes
Concept: Alkene-metal complexes resemble cyclopropane rings
Concept: Ziegler-Natta catalysts polymerize alkenes
Concept: Can't rotate → configurational isomerism denoted E-Z or cis-trans
Concept: Unsaturated fatty acids have bent shape ('unsaturated')
Concept: Cis-trans isomerization breaks stiff pi bond, allowing 180° rotation
Concept: Retinal cis-trans isomerization triggers opsin in the retina (vision)
Concept: Ethylene: simplest alkene (C2H4); plant hormone that stimulates flower development and fruit ripening
Concept: Hydroformylation: aldehydes synthesized industrially by carbonylating alkenes (cobalt + 4 CO ligands)
Concept: Wacker process: ethylene oxidized to acetaldehyde using PdCl2 catalyst, regenerated with CuCl2
Concept: HFC refrigerants with alkene structures have reduced global warming potential
Concept: Conjugated polyenes serve as chromophores; absorbance modeled as particle in a box
Concept: 2000 Nobel (Heeger, MacDiarmid, Shirakawa): conductive polymer containing alkenes (polyacetylene)
Concept: Also known as olefins
Concept: Contain sp2-hybridized carbon atoms
Concept: General formula CnH2n
Concept: Compounds with 1,5-pattern of two alkenes undergo [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement (Cope)
